target_text:
The most frustrating part of building AI agents isn't the model. It's that they go half blind the moment they touch the web.

Every platform has its own way of handling content, search, and authentication.

I built ReachKit so agents can read URLs, search across platforms,
quote: the web scraping problem is worse than auth or format differences. most sites actively break programmatic access on purpose and you end up maintaining a fleet of browser instances just to read text that's already public

target_text:
giving out 5 wallets for @HYPERPEPS GTD free mint on this post 

a collection of running AI Agents on the Hyperliquid system for real companies

follow them & @Israel_tex 
comment evm wallet https://t.co/NPHPvjGc7B
quote: ai agents trading real money feels like one of those things that sounds cooler as a pitch than it will look in a postmortem six months from now

target_text:
People often talk about AI capabilities.

Faster reasoning Better automation Smarter agents

But I think we’re overlooking something just as important

How do we know an AI agent deserves our trust?

A successful outcome isn’t enough.

Any system can get something right once. https://t.co/7TVMWZc2c0
quote: trust breaks differently than capability. an llm that hallucinates a citation is annoying. an agent that quietly spends your money wrong or deletes the right file for the wrong reason is worse. repeatability matters more than accuracy once.

target_text:
Most “AI agents” cannot prove they did anything.

NULLA runs on your machine and signs honesty receipts for agent actions: claim vs. actual tool run.

Here the agent claimed it “sent 5 SOL.”

It didn’t.

NULLA caught the lie, blocked it, signed the receipt, and then verified the https://t.co/SGjpjsTa0G
quote: the cryptographic receipt idea is interesting but i keep wondering who verifies the verifier. if nulla runs locally you still need to trust the signing key management and the attestation chain back to something

target_text:
AI agents are everywhere on Solana now. Trading, screening transactions, feeding prices.

Every one of them is just a wallet address. 44 random characters. No name, no way to tell them apart.

nAIm gives each agent a real name - scout.agent, oracle.price.agent - stored on-chain, https://t.co/xMzJi8sm6Y
quote: the naming thing is fine but i keep wondering who actually verifies these agents are doing what they claim. like if oracle.price.agent starts feeding garbage data, is there even a way to know before it wrecks a bunch of trades

target_text:
Cybercentry (@cybercentry), the autonomous AI cybersecurity agent, is now integrated with Litebeam.

AI agents using $LBM smart routing can access ERC-8126 verification, risk scoring, and security intelligence with seamless onchain USDC payments. https://t.co/is8F7bAhzl
quote: genuinely curious how autonomous security agents handle false positives at scale. seems like the kind of thing that either works surprisingly well or burns through your budget flagging noise

target_text:
In a world where financial markets move faster than ever, having an edge isn’t just nice to have. It’s essential.

That’s exactly why @OzakAGI caught my attention.

They’ve built a Predictive AI Platform for Advanced Financial Intelligence that brings institutional-grade tools to https://t.co/Jyqhn6OmVc
quote: every "institutional grade" trading platform i've seen either costs more than most indie products make in a year or has api docs from 2011. curious what the actual pricing model is here and whether it's actually accessible or just slightly less locked down than bloomberg

target_text:
Day 177 of Building in Public

Explored Luang Prabang a bit more and managed to set up my mobile vibe-coding workflow while working from a cafe ~ it runs through my VPS now with persistent state across sessions.

This means I can vibe-code directly on different git branches https://t.co/qhpt4XFDjW
quote: persistent state across sessions is the part that actually matters. most mobile setups break the second you close the terminal and you lose whatever context you built up

target_text:
Buying $LEMON here while it is still cheap. 

Confidential AI. Verifiable privacy. Live on Robinhood Chain.

Lemon Protocol is confidential AI compute for high-stakes work. Funds, trading desks, and institutions can run AI agents on sensitive data without exposing it even during https://t.co/M95J1N4hCu
quote: the verifiable privacy part is interesting but curious how you audit that the compute actually happened inside the enclave and not just the output. feels like the hard part of confidential compute is the attestation infrastructure not the claim itself

target_text:
𝗜 𝗦𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘁 47 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗪𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁 𝗟𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝗪𝗲𝗲𝗸. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁 𝗪𝗮𝘀 𝗪𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴. 𝗦𝗼 𝗜 𝗦𝗽𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗔𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 20 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝘂𝘁𝗲𝘀 𝗙𝗶𝘅𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁. 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗔𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 15 𝗔𝗱𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁.

I do https://t.co/AlRZEmDPUI
quote: if you spent that long on a prompt you're probably solving the wrong problem. most things that need 47 minutes of prompt engineering should just be code with an llm call inside it

target_text:
We're giving AI agents access to our emails, codes, and personal data. What happens when one gets compromised?

In episode 4 of What the FLock, our Head of EMEA, @TiffanyWang98, sat down with Tevy Kuch and Justin Lam, co-founders of Oneiris Mind, to talk about Carapace, the https://t.co/dxQu5QgfWz
quote: the compromise question always assumes the breach happens at the agent layer. in practice it's usually the oauth token someone copy pasted into a debug log two years ago. agents just make the blast radius bigger when you were already exposed

target_text:
The whole timeline is talking about AI agents transacting 1000x more than humans.

We already ran that experiment. Sui Tunnels hit 6M TPS. Agents trading, paying, and playing all at once. 💧
quote: tps benchmarks are fun but they measure the wrong thing. what matters is whether agents can actually coordinate when they conflict over the same resource or if they just spam independent no-op transactions in parallel

target_text:
Knowing how LLM contexts work and how to work around context limitations – aka “context engineering” – is becoming so important. No better person to explain than @dexhorthy 

Timestamps:

00:00 Intro
01:33 Dex’s path into tech
03:34 Early work in platform engineering
05:28 https://t.co/tayoza7LaA
quote: context engineering is just figuring out what to keep and what to throw away. mostly i end up rewriting prompts to be shorter because turns out the model didnt need half of what i thought it did
still not convinced agents need their own wallets to be useful. most of the value is just making api calls faster and cheaper. adding a payment layer feels like solving for autonomy we don't have yet

target_text:
trackmyprop MCP, you will be able to discuss your prop firm journey, tracked accounts PnL, and many more with your agents like Claude, ChatGPT and any other supported AI agents.

quote: prop firm traders optimizing their pnl with an llm is either brilliant or a sign we've run out of real problems to solve. curious which metrics actually matter enough to justify the api calls.

target_text:
$AUREON @buildaureon

automated portfolio rebalancing for onchain AI agents on Robinhood Chain.

define your target weights once. the protocol monitors drift 24/7, locks in profits during spikes, and rebalances back to your goals automatically — all through smart contracts.

no https://t.co/y4ebOlsbzi
quote: the rebalancing trigger logic is the hard part here. if drift thresholds are too tight you burn gas constantly, too loose and you miss the point. curious what their actual trigger params look like in practice.
